posts.title: Starting this thing
posts.body:
I've been meaning to start writing somewhere that isn't just my notes app for a while now. So — here it is. Nothing fancy, just a place to keep track of the small stuff: what's going on, what I'm thinking about, things worth remembering later.

No real theme or plan here. Just life, as it happens.

More soon.

posts.title: I Thought I Was Learning Websites. I Was Actually Learning How to Learn.
posts.body:
There is a strange moment that arrives when you decide to learn something completely new as an adult.

You don't just battle unfamiliar tools. You battle your own expectations.
At first, every button feels dangerous. Every error message feels permanent. Every tutorial assumes you already know ten things that you've never even heard of. It is easy to believe that everyone else understands what is happening while you are simply trying to keep up.
That was my experience.

I wanted to build a website. Then I discovered domains. Then hosting. Then WordPress. Then GitHub. Then Netlify. Suddenly there were deployments, DNS records, themes, plugins, editors, repositories, and enough terminology to make me wonder whether I had accidentally enrolled in a computer science degree.

The interesting part wasn't the technology.

It was my mindset.

Initially, I wanted to learn everything at once. WordPress, automation, AI, GitHub, SEO, blogging—every shiny new tool looked like the next thing I needed.

But something changed.

Instead of asking, "What else should I learn?" I started asking, "What should I ignore for now?"

That question changed everything.

Now I have one website for publishing, one for experimentation, another for learning, and a clear rule: never experiment on the place that matters most.
More importantly, I have one topic to learn at a time.

This month is WordPress.

Automation can wait.

The internet often celebrates speed. Learn faster. Build faster. Launch faster.

But for many adult learners, progress looks different.

Sometimes progress is deciding not to click another button.

Sometimes it is closing the laptop before making one more unnecessary change.

Sometimes it is accepting that understanding one concept deeply is more valuable than collecting ten unfinished courses.

Learning has become less about technology and more about patience.

Ironically, the biggest lesson I have learned from building websites is not how to deploy one.

It is that confidence is rarely built by avoiding mistakes.

It is built by creating a safe place where mistakes are allowed.

And perhaps that is true far beyond technology.

Sometimes the smartest thing we can build isn't a website.

It's an environment where we are no longer afraid to learn.

posts.title: The Compassion Gap: Why Millions Are Turning to ChatGPT in an Age of Loneliness
posts.body:
There was a time when loneliness meant being physically alone. Today, it often means something far more subtle: being surrounded by people, connected to hundreds online, yet feeling profoundly unseen.
We live in an era of instant communication. We can message anyone across the world in seconds, attend virtual meetings from our homes, and watch the lives of friends unfold on social media in real time. Yet the World Health Organization and public health leaders have warned that loneliness and social isolation are becoming major global health concerns with consequences extending far beyond emotional discomfort. �
World Health Organization +1
The irony is difficult to ignore. We have never been more connected through technology, yet many have never felt more disconnected from one another.
Perhaps the deeper crisis is not loneliness alone. It is what I call the compassion gap.
The Compassion Gap
Loneliness is the absence of meaningful connection. A compassion gap is different. It is the growing distance between a person's need to be heard and society's ability—or willingness—to truly listen.
Modern life has become relentlessly efficient. Productivity is celebrated. Busyness has become a status symbol. Conversations are increasingly transactional. Even among families and close friends, many interactions revolve around solving problems quickly rather than understanding emotions deeply.
People often hesitate before opening up.
"They're already stressed."
"I don't want to be a burden."
"Nobody has time."
As a result, many people stop talking long before they stop hurting.
This gap has become one of the defining emotional realities of modern society.
An Unexpected Listener
Into this landscape has entered an unlikely companion: conversational artificial intelligence.
Millions of people now use ChatGPT and similar AI systems not only for writing emails or solving technical problems but also for discussing anxiety, grief, career uncertainty, parenting challenges, relationship dilemmas, and moments of self-doubt.
This trend surprises many observers.
Why would anyone choose to speak to a machine?
The answer may have less to do with technology than with human psychology.
People are not necessarily looking for a machine that loves them.
They are looking for someone—or something—that will listen.
Unlike most human conversations, AI does not appear impatient. It does not check its phone while you are speaking. It does not interrupt halfway through a sentence. It remembers the context of your conversation and responds immediately, regardless of whether it is 2 p.m. or 2 a.m.
For someone experiencing isolation, those qualities can feel profoundly comforting.
Does AI Feel Compassion?
No.
This distinction matters.
ChatGPT does not experience empathy. It does not feel sadness, joy, affection, or concern. It does not care in the human sense.
What it does remarkably well is simulate compassionate communication.
It acknowledges emotions. It asks clarifying questions. It reflects back what it has understood. It offers structured ways of thinking through problems.
Ironically, many people describe these conversations as feeling more validating than interactions with real people.
That observation should not lead us to conclude that AI has become human.
Instead, it should prompt us to ask a more uncomfortable question:
Have humans become too distracted to offer one another the kind of attention we naturally crave?
Why People Keep Coming Back
Compassion is often confused with advice.
In reality, people frequently need understanding before they need solutions.
A friend may interrupt with stories about their own experience. A colleague may immediately begin solving the problem. Family members may dismiss concerns with phrases such as "Don't overthink it."
AI behaves differently.
It first attempts to understand.
For many users, that simple experience of being heard reduces emotional intensity.
Emerging research supports this possibility. Studies suggest that AI companions can reduce feelings of loneliness for some users, particularly when interactions help them feel heard and understood. At the same time, researchers caution that benefits vary between individuals and should not be viewed as a universal solution. �
arXiv +1
Feeling heard, it seems, may matter almost as much as receiving advice.
The Risk of Artificial Comfort
Every technological advance carries unintended consequences.
If AI becomes our primary emotional companion, we risk replacing relationships that require patience, compromise, and mutual growth with conversations that are always available and often agreeable.
Human relationships are difficult.
Friends disagree.
Partners misunderstand each other.
Family members disappoint us.
These imperfections are precisely what make relationships authentic.
Recent experts have warned that while AI can offer comfort, excessive dependence may weaken opportunities to develop and maintain real-world relationships and interpersonal skills. �
Business Insider +1
AI should therefore be viewed as a supplement—not a substitute—for human connection.
What AI Reveals About Society
The rapid adoption of conversational AI reveals something important.
People are not merely searching for information.
They are searching for presence.
The popularity of AI suggests that society has underestimated the emotional value of attentive listening.
Perhaps the real innovation is not artificial intelligence itself.
Perhaps it is the rediscovery of something humans have always needed: the experience of speaking openly without fear of ridicule, interruption, or judgment.
That should concern us.
If millions of people feel more comfortable sharing their fears with software than with another human being, the issue extends beyond technology.
It becomes a reflection of our communities, workplaces, schools, and families.
Rebuilding Human Compassion
The solution to loneliness cannot be found solely in better algorithms.
It must also be found in better relationships.
Imagine workplaces where managers ask, "How are you really doing?"
Imagine families that replace distracted conversations with uninterrupted attention.
Imagine friendships where listening becomes more important than responding.
Technology can support these efforts, but it cannot replace them.
The U.S. Surgeon General and the World Health Organization both emphasize that meaningful social connection is a cornerstone of health and call for strengthening communities and creating environments that foster genuine human relationships. �
NCBI +1
The Future of Compassion
Artificial intelligence is forcing us to reconsider an old question.
What makes someone feel understood?
The answer may not be intelligence.
It may be attention.
If AI encourages us to remember the importance of listening with patience, asking thoughtful questions, and making others feel seen, then perhaps its greatest contribution will not be technological at all.
It will be cultural.
The future should not be one in which humans compete with AI for compassion.
It should be one in which AI reminds us how deeply compassion has always mattered.
Because the greatest lesson from ChatGPT may not be that machines have learned to sound more human.
It may be that humans are being reminded how to become better listeners again.
